body: The PR description claims a `buildXmlDoc` optimization using `String.Concat`, but this diff does not include that change and `Utils.fs` still uses chained concatenation. Please update the description or include the intended change so reviewers are not validating a non-existent modification.

The explicit `(string * string) list` annotation (and the `List.isEmpty` check) narrows this public helper from the previously inferred sequence of query pairs accepted by the `for` loop. Any consumer or emitted code passing an array/seq of query parameters will stop compiling against the new runtime, so preserve the `seq<string * string>`-compatible signature while adding the fast path.

The new empty-query fast path is not covered for leading-slash OpenAPI paths or `HttpClient.BaseAddress` values with a path component. Existing `createHttpRequest` tests use addresses like `v1/users`, so they don't catch the normalization difference introduced here for schema paths such as `/pets` with a server/basePath like `/api`.

This fast path changes request URI semantics for the normal OpenAPI path shape. `OperationCompiler` passes `schema.Paths` keys directly, and those keys commonly start with `/`; the previous `UriBuilder(...).PathAndQuery.TrimStart('/')` normalized `/pets` to `pets`, but the new empty-query path leaves `/pets`. When combined with an `HttpClient.BaseAddress` that includes a base path, a leading-slash relative URI is resolved from the host root and drops the base path, so operations without query parameters can be sent to the wrong endpoint.

Applied in `5649046`: updated the XML docs so `parseV3Schema` now documents only parsing/validation, and moved the option-specific compile documentation to `compileV3SchemaCoreWithOptions`. No UI changes in this PR, so no screenshot applies.
@copilot apply changes based on [this feedback](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/pull/432#discussion_r3232262150)

The XML doc comment block above `parseV3Schema` describes `provideNullable`/`useDateOnly`/`asAsync` behavior and claims to compile the schema, but `parseV3Schema` only parses and doesn’t take those options. Consider moving/updating this comment so it documents `compileV3SchemaCoreWithOptions` (or rewriting it to describe just parsing) to avoid misleading future test helpers usage.

`compileV3SchemaWithOptions` duplicates the parse/diagnostic/document-null handling logic that already exists in `compileV3SchemaCoreWithOptions`. To avoid the two helpers drifting (e.g., future parse settings changes), consider factoring out a shared `parseV3Schema` helper or extending `compileV3SchemaCoreWithOptions` to accept `ignoreControllerPrefix`/`ignoreOperationId` and calling it from here.

The updated NoMediaType comment says the generated `noData` parameter lets callers pass “any serialisable value”, but the implementation currently ignores `NoData` payloads when building the `HttpRequestMessage` (no `msg.Content` is set for `Some(NoData, _)`). Either adjust the comment to match the behavior (parameter is effectively a placeholder / no body is sent), or change the NoMediaType handling to serialize and send the provided value with an appropriate default media type.

This file already opens `System.Threading` and earlier tests use `typeof<CancellationToken>`. Using `typeof<Threading.CancellationToken>` here is inconsistent and makes the tests noisier; prefer the shorter `typeof<CancellationToken>` for consistency.

This file already opens `System.Threading` and earlier tests use `typeof<CancellationToken>`. Using `typeof<Threading.CancellationToken>` here is inconsistent and makes the tests noisier; prefer the shorter `typeof<CancellationToken>` for consistency.

This file already opens `System.Threading` and earlier tests use `typeof<CancellationToken>`. Using `typeof<Threading.CancellationToken>` here is inconsistent and makes the tests noisier; prefer the shorter `typeof<CancellationToken>` for consistency.

The test name/comment says it verifies the auto-generated method name when `operationId` is missing, but the assertions only check that `types`/`allMethods` are non-empty. This won’t catch regressions in the naming logic; consider asserting that the expected generated method (e.g., `GetItemsCategories`) actually exists (or otherwise asserting the naming rule you intend to guarantee).

The PR description/title say the System.Text.Json constraint is relaxed to ">= 8", but this change introduces an upper bound ("< 11"). If the upper bound is intentional, please document the rationale here and consider applying the same bound in the other Paket groups (Server/Test) that reference System.Text.Json so they don’t later resolve to 11.x independently; otherwise, drop the upper bound to match the stated goal.

`MakeUnique` now calls `methodName.ToLowerInvariant()` directly. If `methodName` is ever null (F# `string` can be null from .NET interop), this will throw, whereas the previous implementation effectively treated null as an empty string via `sprintf` and would not NRE. Consider either normalizing null to "" before lowercasing (preserving previous behavior) or explicitly validating and throwing a clearer error (e.g., `ArgumentNullException`).
Typo/grammar in this comment: "THis lines are" should be corrected (e.g., "These lines are").

The version constraints were relaxed to `>= 8`, which means future major versions (e.g., 11+) can be pulled in automatically on the next `paket install`, potentially breaking CI/builds unexpectedly. Consider using an explicit upper bound (e.g., `>= 8 < 11`) and bumping intentionally per major release if you want stability while still allowing 9/10.

The test name says the request body description appears as a <param> tag, but the assertion only checks that the description text appears somewhere in the XmlDoc. Consider also asserting that the XmlDoc contains a "<param" tag surrounding this text (without depending on the generated parameter name).

This test is intended to verify that description and enum doc are combined with a newline, but it only asserts that both substrings are present. To actually enforce the newline behavior of combineDescAndEnum, assert for the presence of "\n" (or compare against the full expected string).

The test name says enum values are comma-separated, but the assertions only check that each value is present. This would still pass if values were separated by newlines/semicolons or in an unexpected format. Consider asserting the exact joined string (e.g., "Allowed values: active, inactive, pending") or at least that the output contains ", " between the values.

This test doesn’t assert any behavior (it ignores `doc`), so it won’t fail even if `<returns>` handling regresses. Also, the name says “no success response”, but the schema includes a 204 (which `okResponse` treats as a success/2xx response), so either the name/schema is inconsistent or the expected behavior isn’t being verified. Consider (a) asserting the expected `<returns>` output for 204, or (b) changing the schema to only non-2xx responses and asserting there’s no `<returns>` (and no crash).
Same as above: `String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace` handles null, so the explicit `isNull` check can be dropped for a simpler condition.

The null check is redundant here: `String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace` already returns `true` for null. Consider simplifying the condition to a single `if not (String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace schemaObj.Description) then ...` to reduce noise and keep the pattern consistent with other doc checks.

`UseDateOnly=true` doesn’t appear to be a user-facing/static parameter on `OpenApiClientProvider` (it’s an internal `useDateOnly` bool derived from `cfg.SystemRuntimeAssemblyVersion.Major >= 6`). This release note is likely misleading—please reword to reflect the actual condition (e.g., “on .NET 6+ targets/runtimes” or similar) rather than implying a configurable flag.

`resolveHttpMethod` falls back to `method.ToUpper()` (current-culture) and the lookup is case-sensitive, so callers passing e.g. "get" won’t hit the cached `HttpMethod.Get` instance. Consider constructing `standardHttpMethods` with a case-insensitive string comparer (so no normalization allocation is needed) and using `ToUpperInvariant()` for the fallback path to avoid culture-sensitive protocol normalization issues.
```suggestion
    // instances. Uses an ordinal case-insensitive comparer so callers passing different
    // casing (for example, "get") still resolve to the cached standard HttpMethod without
    // allocating a normalized string for lookup.
    let private standardHttpMethods =
        let methods =
            [| HttpMethod.Get
               HttpMethod.Post
               HttpMethod.Put
               HttpMethod.Delete
               HttpMethod("PATCH")
               HttpMethod.Head
               HttpMethod.Options
               HttpMethod.Trace |]

        let dictionary =
            System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary<string, HttpMethod>(StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase)

        methods
        |> Array.iter(fun m -> dictionary[m.Method] <- m)

        System.Collections.ObjectModel.ReadOnlyDictionary<string, HttpMethod>(dictionary)

    let private resolveHttpMethod(method: string) : HttpMethod =
        match standardHttpMethods.TryGetValue method with
        | true, m -> m
        | false, _ -> HttpMethod(method.ToUpperInvariant())
```

`dateOnlyTy` / `timeOnlyTy` are now resolved eagerly during `DefinitionCompiler` construction (when `useDateOnly` is true), even if the schema never uses `format: date`/`time`. If you want to keep the ‘only pay when needed’ behavior while still caching, consider making these `lazy` values (or similar) and forcing them only in the relevant match arms.
```suggestion
    // Resolve DateOnly/TimeOnly lazily and cache the result per compiler instance.
    // This preserves the "only pay when needed" behavior while still avoiding
    // repeated reflection lookups for schemas that actually use date/time formats.
    let dateOnlyTy =
        lazy (
            if useDateOnly then
                System.Type.GetType("System.DateOnly")
                |> Option.ofObj
                |> Option.defaultValue typeof<DateTimeOffset>
            else
                typeof<DateTimeOffset>
        )

    let timeOnlyTy =
        lazy (
            if useDateOnly then
                System.Type.GetType("System.TimeOnly")
                |> Option.ofObj
                |> Option.defaultValue typeof<string>
            else
                typeof<string>
        )
```

PR description says string-enum members get `[JsonPropertyName(originalValue)]`, but the implementation here emits `[JsonStringEnumMemberName]` (with no `JsonPropertyName` fallback). Please update the PR description and/or code so they match, and clarify the intended behavior on STJ<9 / netstandard2.0 where `JsonStringEnumMemberNameAttribute` is not available.

The comment says "Use Dictionary + TryAdd" but the implementation uses `ContainsKey` + `Add` (likely for netstandard2.0 compatibility). Please update the comment to match the code (or switch to `TryAdd` when available) to avoid misleading future maintainers.
```suggestion
            // Use Dictionary with ContainsKey + Add instead of |> dict to safely handle alias values
```

`lookup |> dict` will throw if the enum contains multiple members with the same underlying integral value (alias values), which is legal in .NET enums. That would make `toParam` crash for such enums. Consider building the map with duplicate-handling (e.g., group by key and pick the first/last, or use a mutable Dictionary with `TryAdd`) to keep serialization robust.

`buildEnumSerializer` keys string-enum wire values off `JsonPropertyNameAttribute` on enum fields. If the design-time generator moves to an enum-member attribute/converter that System.Text.Json actually honors (recommended), this runtime lookup should be updated to read the same source of truth; otherwise query/path serialization and JSON serialization will disagree for values that require sanitization (e.g. hyphens).

Enum members are annotated with `getPropertyNameAttribute` (i.e., `JsonPropertyNameAttribute`), but System.Text.Json’s enum converters don’t treat `JsonPropertyName` as an enum-member name override. If the intent is to preserve exact OpenAPI wire values (including hyphens) for JSON bodies/responses, this needs to use an enum-member naming attribute/converter that System.Text.Json respects (or a custom converter).

`JsonStringEnumConverter` does not use `JsonPropertyNameAttribute` on enum members for (de)serialization in System.Text.Json (and this repo targets netstandard2.0 with System.Text.Json 8.0.x per paket.lock). As a result, generated enums with wire values like `"in-active"` will likely fail to deserialize and will serialize using the sanitized member name instead of the OpenAPI wire value. Consider switching to an enum-member-specific mechanism that System.Text.Json actually honors (e.g., `JsonStringEnumMemberNameAttribute` if available in your target, or a custom `JsonConverter`/`JsonConverterFactory` that maps between wire strings and enum values), and apply that converter/attributes instead of `JsonStringEnumConverter` + `JsonPropertyName`.

These tests assert presence of `JsonPropertyNameAttribute` on enum members, but that attribute is not used by System.Text.Json for enum member naming. To validate the intended behavior (wire values with hyphens, etc.), it would be more reliable to add an end-to-end `JsonSerializer.Serialize`/`Deserialize` test for the generated enum type (and/or assert the correct enum-member naming attribute/converter once the implementation is updated).

`Option.defaultValue(value.ToString())` is eager in F#, so `value.ToString()` will be evaluated even when `GetMethod(...).Invoke(...)` succeeds. That adds unnecessary work (and could trigger side effects) on the fallback path. Consider using `Option.defaultWith (fun () -> value.ToString())` or an explicit `match` to only call `ToString()` when the option is `None`.
```suggestion
            |> Option.defaultWith(fun () -> value.ToString())
```

The reviewer is correct — the inner `registerNew` was redundant. Since the enum branch is only reachable when `fromByPathCompiler = true`, the outer `if fromByPathCompiler then registerNew(tyName, tyType)` at the end of `compileBySchema` handles registration consistently with all other branches. Removed in c04afbc.
@copilot review and fix if needed

In the named-enum branch you call `registerNew(tyName, enumTy :> Type)` and then `compileBySchema` will call `registerNew(tyName, tyType)` again immediately after the match when `fromByPathCompiler` is true. This double-registration is redundant (even if `RegisterType` is effectively idempotent here) and makes the control flow harder to follow. Consider removing the inner `registerNew` call and letting the existing post-match `fromByPathCompiler` registration handle it, consistent with the other branches.
```suggestion

```

`toParam` now correctly handles individual enum values, but `toQueryParams` still special-cases only a fixed set of primitive arrays and otherwise falls back to treating the whole array as a single value. If a query parameter becomes `SomeEnum[]` (which this PR enables via `$ref`-able named enums), it will currently serialize as something like `"System.Foo[]"` instead of multiple `name=value` entries. Consider adding an enum-array case in `toQueryParams` that iterates elements and uses `toParam` per element.

For integer enums, if a numeric enum value can’t be parsed as Int32, the code falls back to `intValue` (sequential ordinal). That produces an enum where the literal values don’t match the OpenAPI schema, which is very hard to detect at runtime. Instead of assigning a generated ordinal on parse failure, it would be safer to either skip invalid entries (like the null-handling does) or throw a compilation error that points to the offending enum value.
```suggestion
                                | false, _ ->
                                    failwithf
                                        "Invalid integer enum value '%s' for enum '%s'. Expected a 32-bit integer literal."
                                        originalStr
                                        tyName
```

Enum generation currently hard-codes the underlying type to int32, but elsewhere in compileBySchema integer schemas map to int64 when `format: int64` (see existing mapping further down). For named integer enums with `format: int64` (or values outside Int32 range), this will either overflow or silently miscompile. Consider selecting the underlying CLR type based on `schemaObj.Format` (e.g., int64 for `int64`) and parsing enum literals with Int64.TryParse when appropriate (or failing fast if a value can’t be represented).
Changed `directory` from `"/"` to `"/.config"` in commit be79d6a to scope the NuGet Dependabot entry to only the dotnet-tools.json manifest.
@copilot apply changes based on [this feedback](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/pull/407#discussion_r3142440167)

The new NuGet entry uses `directory: "/"`, which will cause Dependabot to scan/update all NuGet manifests under the repo root (e.g., `*.fsproj`), not just `.config/dotnet-tools.json` as described. If the intent is to track only dotnet global tools, scope this entry to the tools manifest directory (e.g., `directory: "/.config"`) or add an `ignore`/separate update entry to prevent unintended NuGet dependency PRs.

SSRF protection can be bypassed via HTTP redirects: HttpClientHandler follows redirects by default, but validateSchemaUrl is only applied to the initial URI. Consider setting handler.AllowAutoRedirect = false (or manually handling 3xx with re-validation of each Location target) when SSRF protection is enabled, so redirects can’t jump to http:// or private/loopback hosts.
```suggestion
            // SECURITY: Prevent redirect-based SSRF bypasses when SSRF protection is enabled.
            use handler = new HttpClientHandler(UseDefaultCredentials = false, AllowAutoRedirect = ignoreSsrfProtection)
```

`Choice2Of2 e -> failwith(e.ToString())` rethrows as a new exception and loses the original exception type + stack trace, which makes diagnosing schema fetch failures harder. Prefer re-raising the original exception (or preserving stack trace via `reraise`/`raise`) and only wrap when adding meaningful context.
```suggestion
                | Choice2Of2 e -> e.Reraise()
```

`Async.RunSynchronously` inside an `async {}` block blocks the current thread and can deadlock under a synchronization context. Since this code is already in an async workflow, read `ex.Content` with `let!`/`Async.AwaitTask` and structure the match to return asynchronously rather than forcing a synchronous wait.
```suggestion
            return!
                async {
                    match res with
                    | Choice1Of2 x -> return x
                    | Choice2Of2(:? Swagger.OpenApiException as ex) when not <| isNull ex.Content ->
                        let! content =
                            ex.Content.ReadAsStringAsync()
                            |> Async.AwaitTask

                        return
                            if String.IsNullOrEmpty content then
                                ex.Reraise()
                            else
                                content
                    | Choice2Of2(:? WebException as wex) when not <| isNull wex.Response ->
                        use stream = wex.Response.GetResponseStream()
                        use reader = new StreamReader(stream)
                        let err = reader.ReadToEnd()

                        return
                            if String.IsNullOrEmpty err then
                                wex.Reraise()
                            else
                                err.ToString()
                    | Choice2Of2 e -> return failwith(e.ToString())
                }
```

`HttpRequestMessage` (and the `HttpResponseMessage` from `SendAsync`) are not disposed. This can leak sockets/handlers especially on exceptions (e.g., if `validateContentType` throws before the response is GC’d). Consider using `use request = ...` and `use! response = ...` (or otherwise ensuring both request/response are disposed) while still reading the body.

`unwrapFSharpOption` calls `optionValueCache.GetOrAdd(ty, fun t -> t.GetProperty("Value"))`, which allocates a new lambda on every invocation. Since this PR already introduced the hoisted `optionValueFactory` specifically to avoid per-call allocations, consider reusing `optionValueFactory` here as well to keep the hot form-encoding path allocation-free after the cache is populated (and to match the comment about reusing the cache/factory).
```suggestion
                let prop = optionValueCache.GetOrAdd(ty, optionValueFactory)
```

Fixed in commit `2502fbb`. Both factory delegates are now pre-allocated as module-level `let` bindings (`optionTagReaderFactory` and `optionValueFactory`), so `GetOrAdd` no longer constructs a new `Func`/lambda on every call through the hot path.
@copilot apply changes based on [this feedback](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/pull/399#discussion_r3141509400)

`optionTagReaderCache.GetOrAdd(ty, System.Func<...>(makeOptionTagReader))` constructs a new `System.Func` on every `toParam` call, even after the tag reader is cached. This adds per-call allocations on the hot path and undermines the “allocation-free after 2nd call” goal. Consider hoisting the factory delegate into a `let` binding (or using an overload that doesn't require re-creating the delegate) so repeated calls don't allocate.

This test is intended to ensure non-enum parameters don’t append an "Allowed values:" hint, but it currently allows `doc = None` even though the schema includes an operation `summary` (so `OperationCompiler` should always generate non-empty XmlDoc). Tighten the assertion to require `doc.IsSome` and verify the summary is present, then assert it does not contain "Allowed values:".
```suggestion
    doc.IsSome |> shouldEqual true
    doc.Value |> shouldContainText "Health check"
    doc.Value |> shouldNotContainText "Allowed values:"
```

Updated in e2e883b: I renamed the test to `required string date format maps to DateTimeOffset when useDateOnly is false` to make the expectation explicit and remove ambiguity. No UI changes in this test-only update.
@copilot apply changes based on [this feedback](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/pull/397#discussion_r3128163872)

Now that there is a useDateOnly=true variant for `format: date`, the existing test `required string date format maps to DateTimeOffset` becomes ambiguous (it only holds for useDateOnly=false via `compilePropertyType`). Consider renaming that earlier test to explicitly mention `when useDateOnly is false` for clarity and to prevent future confusion.

Applied in 13da110. I added regression tests that verify `TimeOnly` is serialized consistently for both `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` and multipart form fields (`HH:mm:ss.FFFFFFF` behavior), so these paths are now explicitly covered alongside query/path/header formatting.
@copilot apply changes based on [this feedback](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/pull/394#discussion_r3123239597)

TimeOnly support was added to toParam/toQueryParams, but form-encoding helpers in this same module still stringify values via .ToString() (e.g., toFormUrlEncodedContent and the non-stream branch of toMultipartFormDataContent). If a `format: time` parameter is sent via `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` or multipart fields, serialization will be culture-dependent (often omitting seconds) and won’t match the new "HH:mm:ss.FFFFFFF" behavior. Consider routing those code paths through `toParam` (or a shared formatting function) so TimeOnly/DateOnly are consistently encoded across query/path/header/form/multipart parameters.

Applied in 9788b11. I added the missing form-content regression coverage: DateOnly serialization checks for both `toFormUrlEncodedContent` and `toMultipartFormDataContent`, plus omission tests where `toParam` returns null (nested option case). No UI changes in this PR, so no screenshot is applicable.
@copilot apply changes based on [this feedback](https://github.com/fsprojects/SwaggerProvider/pull/395#discussion_r3122415236)

The PR description calls out incorrect `DateOnly` serialization (and that `toParam` returning `null` for `None` options should cause form entries to be skipped), but the added tests only cover `DateTime`/`DateTimeOffset`. Consider adding analogous assertions for `DateOnly` in both `toFormUrlEncodedContent` and `toMultipartFormDataContent`, and a small test that `None` values are omitted when passed directly (to lock in the documented trade-off).

This multipart DateTimeOffset test only checks for the date substring, which may pass even if the time/offset are not serialized correctly. Consider asserting the body equals `dto.ToString("O")` so the test actually verifies ISO 8601 round-trip serialization for DateTimeOffset.
```suggestion
            body |> shouldEqual (dto.ToString("O"))
```

This multipart DateTime test only asserts the body contains the date portion, which doesn’t guarantee the full value is serialized in ISO 8601 round-trip format. Since the part is a `StringContent`, you can assert the body equals `dt.ToString("O")` to ensure the time, fractional seconds, and timezone are preserved.

The assertions in this DateTime form-url-encoding test are substring-based and don’t verify the actual decoded parameter value matches the expected round-trip ISO 8601 format (e.g., it doesn’t check the required 'T' separator, fractional seconds, or 'Z'). This can let locale-dependent formats slip through in cultures that also use `yyyy-MM-dd`. Consider parsing the returned body as a query string and asserting the `ts` value equals `dt.ToString("O")` (after decoding), rather than checking a few substrings.

The doc comment for `useDateOnly` says it controls whether `date/date-time/time` formats can map to `DateOnly/TimeOnly`, but `date-time` still always maps to `DateTimeOffset` (see `DefinitionCompiler`’s `"date-time"` case). Consider updating the comment to avoid implying `date-time` ever maps to DateOnly/TimeOnly.
```suggestion
/// `useDateOnly` controls whether `date` and `time` formats map to DateOnly and TimeOnly types.
```

This new schema test only validates the fallback behavior when `useDateOnly=false` (because `compileV3SchemaCore` hard-codes `useDateOnly` to false in `Schema.TestHelpers`). The new functionality is the `useDateOnly=true` mapping to `System.TimeOnly`, but there’s currently no test coverage that asserts `format: time` maps to `TimeOnly` when `useDateOnly` is enabled. Consider adding a helper that allows compiling with `useDateOnly=true` (similar to `compilePropertyTypeWith`) and adding an assertion for `typeof<TimeOnly>`.
```suggestion
[<Fact>]
let ``required string time format maps to TimeOnly when useDateOnly is true``() =
    let ty =
        compilePropertyTypeWith "          type: string\n          format: time\n" true true

    ty |> shouldEqual typeof<TimeOnly>

[<Fact>]
```

PR description claims there are “2 `Schema.TypeMappingTests` for the new `format: time` mapping”, but this file only adds a single `format: time` test (and it asserts the fallback-to-string case). If a `useDateOnly=true` → `TimeOnly` mapping test was intended, it appears to be missing from the PR.

`tryFormatDateOnly` falls back to `value.ToString()` when it can’t find the `ToString(string)` overload. That fallback reintroduces the original culture-dependent behavior this PR is trying to eliminate (and could happen if the available overloads differ across target runtimes). Consider instead resolving an overload that’s guaranteed to exist (e.g., `ToString(string, IFormatProvider)` with `InvariantCulture`), or formatting via `IFormattable`/`ISpanFormattable` so DateOnly always serializes as `yyyy-MM-dd` without relying on the 1-arg overload being present.
```suggestion
                let invariantCulture = System.Globalization.CultureInfo.InvariantCulture
                let dateOnlyFormat = "yyyy-MM-dd"

                match ty.GetMethod("ToString", [| typeof<string>; typeof<IFormatProvider> |]) |> Option.ofObj with
                | Some methodInfo ->
                    Some(methodInfo.Invoke(value, [| box dateOnlyFormat; box invariantCulture |]) :?> string)
                | None ->
                    match ty.GetMethod("ToString", [| typeof<string> |]) |> Option.ofObj with
                    | Some methodInfo -> Some(methodInfo.Invoke(value, [| box dateOnlyFormat |]) :?> string)
                    | None ->
                        match value with
                        | :? IFormattable as formattable -> Some(formattable.ToString(dateOnlyFormat, invariantCulture))
                        | _ -> None
```

`toQueryParams` handles `array<Option<Guid>>` by calling `toStrArray`, which will stringify the option cases (e.g., `Some ...` / `None`) instead of unwrapping and skipping `None` items like the other `array<Option<...>>` branches do. This should use the option-aware path (e.g., `toStrArrayOpt`) and ideally add a unit test covering `Option<Guid>[]` with both `Some` and `None` elements.
```suggestion
            | :? array<Option<Guid>> as xs -> xs |> toStrArrayOpt name
```

The new `DateOnly` tests cover `DateOnly` and `DateOnly option`, but not the `Nullable<DateOnly>` shape that is produced when using `PreferNullable=true`. Adding assertions for `toParam (box (Nullable<DateOnly>(...)))`, `toParam (box (Nullable<DateOnly>()))`, and the equivalent `toQueryParams` cases would prevent regressions once nullable handling is added.
```suggestion
    [<Fact>]
    let ``toQueryParams handles Nullable<DateOnly> with value``() =
        let d = Nullable(DateOnly(2025, 6, 15))
        let result = toQueryParams "date" (box d) stubClient
        result |> shouldEqual [ ("date", "2025-06-15") ]

    [<Fact>]
    let ``toQueryParams handles Nullable<DateOnly> without value``() =
        let result = toQueryParams "date" (box(Nullable<DateOnly>())) stubClient
        result |> shouldEqual []

    [<Fact>]
    let ``toQueryParams skips None items in Option<DateOnly> array``() =
        let d = DateOnly(2025, 3, 1)
        let values: Option<DateOnly>[] = [| Some d; None |]
        let result = toQueryParams "dates" (box values) stubClient
        result |> shouldEqual [ ("dates", "2025-03-01") ]

    [<Fact>]
    let ``toQueryParams skips empty items in Nullable<DateOnly> array``() =
        let values: Nullable<DateOnly>[] = [| Nullable(DateOnly(2025, 3, 1)); Nullable() |]
        let result = toQueryParams "dates" (box values) stubClient
        result |> shouldEqual [ ("dates", "2025-03-01") ]
```

`DateOnly` values can also arrive boxed as `Nullable<DateOnly>` when the provider is instantiated with `PreferNullable=true` (optional value-type params/properties are wrapped in `Nullable<_>`). `tryFormatDateOnly` / `toParam` / `toQueryParams` currently only recognize the non-nullable `System.DateOnly`, so `Nullable<DateOnly>.ToString()` will be used (culture-dependent) and `HasValue=false` will serialize as an empty string instead of being omitted/null. Consider unwrapping `Nullable<_>` (when the underlying type is DateOnly) and treating `HasValue=false` as None, then formatting the underlying DateOnly with the ISO/round-trip format.

The CI dependency bump entry doesn’t include the PR reference(s), unlike most other bullets in this release notes file. Since this beta02 section is meant to capture specific merged PRs, consider appending the relevant PR number(s) (e.g. #387–#390) or splitting into separate bullets so readers can trace each bump back to a PR.

Updated the PR metadata to match the current diff in 785f4d0: title now reflects the `CallAsync` return-type refactor, and description now documents only the 3 applied file changes (no `LastResponseHeaders` feature).
@copilot update pr title and description based on the diff of applied changes.

This test now keeps the returned `HttpResponseMessage` undisposed. Since `CallAsync` returns `HttpResponseMessage`, it should be disposed in the success path (e.g., `use! response = ...`) to match typical HttpClient usage and avoid resource leaks in the test suite.

PR description says generated methods get an *optional* `cancellationToken: CancellationToken = CancellationToken.None`, but the implementation here generates two overloads (with/without CT). Either update the PR description to match the overload-based API, or switch the generated parameter to be optional if that's still the intended surface area.

`invokeCode` treats any argument variable named exactly `cancellationToken` as the cancellation token. This will break for specs that legitimately define a parameter named `cancellationToken` (it will be removed from the OpenAPI parameter list and never applied to path/query/header/cookie).

If you keep the overload approach, extract the CT argument by referencing the actual `ProvidedParameter` you added (or a generated unique name as suggested above), rather than hard-coding the variable name string.

In the CT overload, `CancellationToken` is appended after `providedParameters`, which already contains optional OpenAPI parameters (those with `DefaultParameterValue`). That produces a signature with required parameters after optional ones (hard/awkward to call from many .NET languages) and makes it difficult to pass a CT without also specifying every preceding optional param.

Also, the CT parameter is always named `cancellationToken` and is appended without checking for collisions with generated OpenAPI parameter names. If the spec defines a parameter that becomes `cancellationToken`, you'll end up with duplicate parameter names and/or mis-binding.

Consider: (1) choose a CT parameter name that cannot collide (generate a unique name from the current used-name set), and (2) order parameters as required OpenAPI params, then CT, then optional OpenAPI params (or provide an additional overload that accepts CT alongside only the required params).

The added CancellationToken parameter is always named "cancellationToken" without checking whether an OpenAPI parameter/body field already normalizes to the same name. That can (1) create duplicate parameter names when includeCancellationToken=true, and (2) when includeCancellationToken=false, cause a real OpenAPI parameter named "cancellationToken" to be misinterpreted as the special token in invokeCode and be omitted from the request. Make the CancellationToken parameter name unique against the existing providedParameters (or adjust the invokeCode matching so it only treats the argument as a CT when it doesn’t correspond to an OpenAPI parameter).
```suggestion
                    let existingNames =
                        providedParameters
                        |> List.map (fun (p: ProvidedParameter) -> p.Name)
                        |> Set.ofList

                    let rec uniqueName baseName idx =
                        let candidate =
                            if idx = 0 then baseName
                            else baseName + string idx

                        if existingNames.Contains candidate then
                            uniqueName baseName (idx + 1)
                        else
                            candidate

                    let ctName = uniqueName "cancellationToken" 0

                    let ctParam =
                        ProvidedParameter(ctName, typeof<Threading.CancellationToken>)
```

Refactor so the operation’s parameter/return type compilation happens once and then create the 2 ProvidedMethod overloads from the shared compiled metadata (only appending the CancellationToken parameter for the second overload).
